volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Stichus (Section 1.2.109 - Section 1.3.231)

sections.label: Section 1.2.109
sections.original: Facile invenies et peiorem et peius moratam , pater ,
sections.translation: You ’ ll easily find , father , one both worse and of worse morals

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.original: quam illa fuit : meliorem neque tu reperies neque sol videt .
sections.translation: than she was ; one better you ’ ll neither find nor does the sun behold .

sections.label: Section 1.2.111
sections.original: At ego ex te éxquaero atque ex istac tua sorore .
sections.translation: But I ’ m making the enquiry of you , and of this sister of yours .

sections.label: Section 1.2.111b
sections.original: Edepol pater ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , father , I know how they should be , if they are to be such as I think right .

sections.label: Section 1.2.113
sections.original: Volo scire ergo , ut aequom censes .
sections.translation: I wish , then , to know what you do think right .

sections.label: Section 1.2.113b
sections.original: Vt , per urbem quom ambulent ,
sections.translation: That when they walk through the city , they should shut the mouths of all , so that none can speak ill of them with good reason .

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: Dic vicissim nunciam tu .
sections.translation: And now speak you in your turn .

sections.label: Section 1.2.115b
sections.original: Quid vis tibi dicam , pater ?
sections.translation: What do you wish that I should speak to you about , father ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.116
sections.original: Vbi facillime spectatur mulier , quae ingenio est bono ?
sections.translation: How is the woman most easily distinguished , who is of a good disposition ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.117
sections.original: Quoi male faciundi est potestas , quae ne id faciat temperat .
sections.translation: When she , who has the power of doing ill , refrains from doing so .

sections.label: Section 1.2.118
sections.original: Hau male istuc . age tu altera , utra sit condicio pensior ,
sections.translation: Not bad that . ( To PAMPHILA .) Come , say you , which choice is the preferable , to marry a maiden or a widow ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.119b
sections.original: Quanta méa sapientiast ,
sections.translation: So far as my skill extends ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.120
sections.original: ex malis multis malum quod minimumst , id minimest malum .
sections.translation: of many evils [*] , that which is the least evil , the same is the least an evil .

sections.label: Section 1.2.121
sections.original: Qui potest mulier vitare vitiis ?
sections.translation: He that can avoid the women , let him avoid them ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.121b
sections.original: Vt cottidie
sections.translation: so that each day he takes care , the day before , not to do that which , the day after , he may regret .

sections.label: Section 1.2.123
sections.original: Quae tibi muliér videtur multo sapientissuma ?
sections.translation: What sort of woman , pray , seems to you by far the wisest ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.124
sections.original: Quae tamen , cum res secundae sunt , se poterit noscere ,
sections.translation: She who , when affairs are prosperous , shall still be able to know herself ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: et illa quae aequo animo patietur sibi esse peius quam fuit .
sections.translation: and who with equanimity can endure it to be worse with her than it has been .

sections.label: Section 1.2.126
sections.original: Edepol vos lepide temptavi vostrumque ingenium ingeni .
sections.translation: By my troth , in merry mood have I been trying the bent of your dispositions . But ’ tis this for which I am come to you , and for which I wished to meet you both . My friends are advising me to the effect that I should remove you hence to my own house .

sections.label: Section 1.2.129
sections.original: At enim nos , quarum res agitur , aliter auctores sumus .
sections.translation: But still , we , whose interests are concerned , are advising you quite otherwise .

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: nam aut olim , nisi tibi placebant , non datas oportuit ,
sections.translation: For either , father , we ought not formerly to have been bestowed in marriage , unless our husbands pleased you , or , it is not right for us now to be taken away when they are absent .

sections.label: Section 1.2.132
sections.original: Vosne ego patiar cum mendicis nuptas me vivo viris ?
sections.translation: And shall I suffer you while I am alive to remain married to men who are beggars ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.133
sections.original: Placet ille meus mihi mendicus : suos rex reginae placet .
sections.translation: This beggar of mine is agreable to me ; her own king is agreable [*] to the queen . In poverty have I the same feelings that once I had in riches .

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: Vosne latrones et mendicos homines magni penditis ?
sections.translation: And do you set such high value on thieves and beggars ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.136
sections.original: non tu me argento dedisti , opinor , nuptum , sed viro .
sections.translation: You did not , as I think , give me in marriage to the money , but to the man .

sections.label: Section 1.2.137
sections.original: Quid illos exspectatis , quí abhinc iam abierunt triennium ?
sections.translation: Why are you still in expectation of those who have been absent for now three years ? Why don ’ t you accept an eligible match [*] in place of a very bad one ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.139
sections.original: Stultitiast , pater , venatum ducere invitas canes .
sections.translation: ’ Tis folly , father , to lead unwilling dogs to hunt .

sections.label: Section 1.2.140
sections.original: hostis est uxor , invita quae viro nuptum datur .
sections.translation: That wife is an enemy , who is given to a man in marriage against her will .

sections.label: Section 1.2.141
sections.original: Certumne est neutram vostrarum persequi imperium patris ?
sections.translation: Are you then determined that neither of you will obey the command of your father ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.142
sections.original: Persequimur , nam quo dedisti nuptum , abire nolumus .
sections.translation: We do obey ; for where you gave us in marriage , thence are we unwilling to depart .

sections.label: Section 1.2.143
sections.original: Bene valete . ibo atque amicis vostra consilia eloquar .
sections.translation: Kindly good b ’ ye ; I ’ ll go and tell my friends your resolutions .

sections.label: Section 1.2.144
sections.original: Probiores credo arbitrabunt , si probis narraveris .
sections.translation: They will , I doubt not , think us the more honorable , if you tell them to honorable men .

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: Curate igitur familiarem rem ut potestis .
sections.translation: Take you care , then , of their domestic concerns , the best way that you can .

sections.label: Section 1.2.145b
sections.original: Optume ,
sections.translation: Now you gratify us , when you direct us aright : now we will hearken to you . Now , sister , let ’ s go indoors .

sections.label: Section 1.2.147b
sections.original: Ímmo intervisam domum .
sections.translation: Well , first I ’ ll take a look at home . If , perchance , any news should come to you from your husband , take you care that I know it .

sections.label: Section 1.2.149
sections.original: Neque ego te celabo , neque tu me celassis quod scias .
sections.translation: Neither will I conceal it from you , nor do you conceal from me what you may know .

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: eho , Crocotium , i , parasitum Gelasimum huc arcessito ,
sections.translation: ( Calls at the door of her house .) Ho there , Crocotium [*] , go , fetch hither Gelasimus , the Parasite ; bring him here with you . For , i ’ faith , I wish to send him to the harbour , to see if , perchance , any ship from Asia [*] has arrived there yesterday or to-day . But , one servant has been sitting at the harbour whole days in waiting ; still , however , I wish it to be visited every now and then . Make haste , and return immediately .

sections.label: Section 1.3.155
sections.original: Famem ego fuisse suspicor matrem mihi ,
sections.translation: I do suspect that Famine was my mother ; for since I was born I have never been filled with victuals . And no man could better return the favour to his mother , than do I right unwillingly return it to my mother , Famine . For in her womb , for ten months she bore me ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.160
sections.original: at ego illam in alvo gesto plus annos decem .
sections.translation: whereas I have been carrying her for more than ten years in my stomach . She , too , carried me but a little child , wherefore I judge that she endured the less labour ; in my stomach no little Famine do I bear , but of full growth , i ’ faith , and extremely heavy .

sections.label: Section 1.3.165
sections.original: uteri dolores mihi oboriuntur cotidie , †
sections.translation: The labour-pains arise with me each day , but I ’ m unable to bring forth my mother , nor know I what to do . I ’ ve often heard it so said that the elephant is wont [*] to be pregnant ten whole years ; for sure this hunger of mine is of its breed .

sections.label: Section 1.3.170
sections.original: nam iam complures annos utero haeret meo .
sections.translation: For now for many a year has it been clinging to , my inside . Now , if any person wants a droll fellow , I am on sale , with all my equipage : of a filling-up for these chasms am I in search . When little , my father gave me the name of Gelasimus [*] ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.175
sections.original: quia inde iam a pausillo puero ridiculus fui . †
sections.translation: because , even from a tiny child , I was a droll chap . By reason of poverty , in fact , did I acquire this name , because , it was poverty that made me to be a droll ; for whenever she reaches a person , she instructs him thoroughly in every art . My father used to say that I was born when provisions were dear ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.180
sections.original: propterea , credo , nunc essurio ácrius .
sections.translation: for that reason , I do believe , I am now the more sharply set . But on our family such complacence has been bestowed — I am in the habit of refusing no person , if any one asks me out to eat . One form of expression has most unfortunately died away with people , and one , i ’ faith , most beseeming and most elegant to my thinking ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.185
sections.original: qua ante utebantur : “ véni illo ad cenam , sic face ,
sections.translation: which formerly they employed : Come here to dinner — do so — really , do promise — don ’ t make any difficulties — is it convenient ?— I wish it to be so , I say ; I ’ ll not part with you unless you come . But now , in the present day , they have found a substitute for these expressions — a saying , by my faith , truly right worthless and most vile :

sections.label: Section 1.3.190
sections.original: vocem te ad cenam , nisi egomet cenem foris .
sections.translation: I ’ d invite you to dinner , were I not dining ; out myself . I ’ faith , I wish the very loins of that phrase broken , that it mayn ’ t repeat its perjury if he does dine at his own house . These phrases reduce me to learn foreign habits [*] , and to spare the necessity for an auctioneer ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.195
sections.original: itaque auctionem praedicem , ipse ut venditem .
sections.translation: and so proclaim the auction , and put myself up for sale .

sections.label: Section 1.3.196
sections.original: Hic illést parasitus , quem arcessitum missa sum .
sections.translation: This is the Parasite , whom I ’ ve been sent to fetch . I ’ ll listen to what he ’ s saying , before I accost him .

sections.label: Section 1.3.198
sections.original: Sed curiosi sunt hic complures mali ,
sections.translation: Now there are a good many curious mischief-makers here , who , with extreme zeal , busy themselves with the affairs of other people ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.200
sections.original: quibus ipsis nullast res , quam procurent , sua :
sections.translation: and who have themselves no affairs of their own to busy themselves with . They , when they know that any one is about to have an auction , go forthwith and sift out what ’ s the reason ; whether a debt compels it , or whether he has purchased a farm ; or whether , on a divorce , her marriage-portion is to be repaid to his wife [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.3.205
sections.original: eos ómnis tametsi hercle haud indignos iudico
sections.translation: All these , although , i ’ faith , I don ’ t judge them undeserving , in their most wretched state , to go toiling on , I don ’ t care about . I ’ ll proclaim the reason of my auction , that they may rejoice in my mishaps , for there ’ s no person a busybody but what he ’ s ill-natured too . Very great mishaps , alas ! have befallen wretched me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.210
sections.original: ita me mancupia miserum adfecerunt male ,
sections.translation: So dreadfully afflicted has my property [*] rendered me : my many drinking-bouts are dead and gone ; how many dinners , too , that I ’ ve bewailed , are dead ! how many a draught of honeyed wine ; how many breakfasts , too , that I have lost within these last three years !

sections.label: Section 1.3.215
sections.original: prae maerore adeo miser atque aegritudine
sections.translation: In my wretchedness , for very grief and vexation have I quite grown old . I ’ m almost dead with hunger .

sections.label: Section 1.3.217
sections.original: Ridiculus aeque nullus est quando esurit .
sections.translation: There ’ s no one such a droll , as he is when he is hungry .

sections.label: Section 1.3.218
sections.original: Nunc auctionem facere decretumst mihi :
sections.translation: Now am I resolved that I ’ ll make a sale : out of doors [*] am I obliged to sell whatever I possess .

sections.label: Section 1.3.220
sections.original: adeste sultis , praeda erit praesentium .
sections.translation: Attend , if you please ; the bargains will be for those who are present . I ’ ve funny bon mots [*] to sell . Come , bid your price . Who bids a dinner ? Does any one bid a breakfast ? They ’ ll cost vou an Herculean breakfast [*] or dinner . Ho , there ! ( to one of the SPECTATORS ) did you nod to me ? No one will offer you better —

sections.label: Section 1.3.225
sections.original: nulli meliores esse parasito sinam .
sections.translation: I won ’ t allow that any Parasite has better

sections.label: Section 1.3.226
sections.original: vel unctiones Graecas sudatorias
sections.translation: for the Greek unguents [*] at the sweating-baths [*] ; delicate after-dinner powders [*] ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.228
sections.original: cavillátiones , adsentatiunculas
sections.translation: quibbles , cajoleries , and parasitical white lies [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.3.230
sections.original: robiginosam strigilem , ampullam rubidam ,
sections.translation: I ’ m selling a rusty flesh-scraper [*] , too ; a rusty-coloured brown bottle [*]

sections.label: Section 1.3.231
sections.original: parasitum inanem quo recondas reliquias .
sections.translation: an empty Parasite as well ( pointing to himself ) , in whom to lay by your scraps . ’ Tis needful that these should be sold at once for as much as they can ; that , if I offer the tenth part . to Hercules [*] , on that account it may be greater ⋯
